course_title: Writing the Genre Novel: Heroes, Lovers, and Monsters
course_description:
This course considers the kinds of fiction that are usually described as "genre" or "popular" works. It provides a practice-led exploration and analysis of a range of significant genre texts and techniques. The emphasis is on the genre novel, and students will be asked to workshop drafts, critically analyse text, and write an opening chapter in a genre of their choosing. Students will also consider the theory of genre fiction, and how genres operate in the literary marketplace.

Purpose of the Course

This course provides a practice-led exploration and analysis of a range of significant genre fiction texts and writing techniques. It is also designed to stimulate your curiosity, give you a chance to meet kindred spirits, and develop an understanding of the market for commercial fiction.

Course Goals

On completion of the course, you will be familiar with a wide range of ideas and approaches suitable for creative writing projects, and will have gained a thorough understanding of the workings of the genre novel. You will have gained transferable skills in creativity, communication, planning, and imagining alternate contexts, viewpoints, and events.

Teaching and Learning Modes

WRIT2050 is taught as a weekly 1-hour lecture and 2-hour tutorial. The lectures are delivered live. All lecture and tutorial material relates to assessment. You would be at a significant disadvantage if you did not engage with your lecturer and tutor.ﾠ

Expectations and Obligations

timetabling.commarts@enquire.uq.edu.au
from your
UQ student email
with:
Your name
Your student ID
The course code
A list of three tutorial preferences (in order of preference)
Reason for the change – e.g. timetable clash, elite athlete status, SAP
Teaching staff do not have access to change tutorials or help with timetables; all timetabling changes must be processed through the Timetabling Team.
course_aims: This course considers the kinds of fiction that are usually described as "genre"or "popular" works. It provides a practice-led exploration and analysis of a range of significant genre texts and techniques. The emphasis is on the genre novel, and students will be asked to workshop a synopsis and write an opening chapter in a genre of their choosing. Students will also consider the theory of genre fiction, and how genres operate in the literary marketplace. Genres studied may include young adult, fantasy, horror, romance, thriller, historical and science fiction.

learning_outcomes.description: Conceptualise genre as it relates to fiction and to the literary marketplace.
Develop critical analysis skills and apply them to creative practice.
Demonstrate writing fluency and time management skills essential for creative writing practice, including planning, researching, drafting, and revising under deadline constraints.

assessment_details.title: Reflection Logbooks
assessment_details.category: Notebook/ Logbook, Participation/ Student contribution, Reflection
assessment_details.other_conditions: Peer assessment factor, Secure. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: Across the semester, you will use a template to complete 8 short in-class written reflections, one in each tutorial. You will receive a different weekly prompt during each tutorial. Your best 6 scores will be counted toward your final grade. The logbooks will ask you to reflect on what you learned in the lecture, tutorial, peer workshops and/or in your own reading for that week's topic ie characterisation, plotting, building tension. Purpose These reflections help you: Consolidate what you learned in that week’s lecture, tutorial and peer workshopping sessions. Demonstrate the changes or adjustments you made to your practice or thinking Build employability skills in rapid analysis, critical thinking, insight generation, concise writing and academic reflection Format: Handwritten in class Template provided Tutorial prompt given on the day You will have 20 minutes for each reflection Approx. 200-300 words in total Note: Each weekly response is marked out of 5% 6 highest-scoring reflections contribute to the final 30% Marking focuses on: Insight and depth of thinking Quality of analysis and reflection Engagement with lecture/tutorial/workshopping content Application of learning to your own writing Completion of all parts of the weekly prompt I'll go through logbook results during selected tutorials Your active participation in peer workshopping is essential and forms part of your overall learning and development in this course. The in-person weekly reflection logbook requires you to show how workshopping is helping you improve your own writing. You will submit a longer version of this same draft as your final submission. Note: This assessment has a pass/fail hurdle. Peer Workshopping Requirements You will submit a draft of your creative work for peer workshopping early in the course (up to 1,000 words). In return, you will also read and workshop your peers’ drafts. This is a weekly requirement and an important part of developing your craft. The set reading list for this course is intentionally short to ensure you have sufficient time each week to: Read your peers’ work carefully Provide thoughtful, constructive feedback Apply feedback you receive to your own revisions If you have an SAPD that allows changes to this assessment, please contact me directly at the start of semester. This assessment task is to be completed in-person, weekly. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Submit handwritten logbook entry to tutor during the tutorial.
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
If you have an SAPD that allows changes to this assessment, please contact me directly.
Late submission
You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late .
If you do not attend tutorials to complete 6 Logbook entries, you will forfeit the whole 5% per logbook entry (or per week missed). There are 8 opportunities to finish the required 6 logbook entries.
assessment_details.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessment_details.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late .

assessment_details.title: Analysis of Writing Craft
assessment_details.due_date: 24/04/2026 4:00 pm Submit via TurnItIn through Blackboard
assessment_details.other_conditions: Student specific. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: Read the excerpt provided, then identify one craft technique from the list that is used in the excerpt. Then respond to the five questions below ensuring you remain within the indicated word limits. Total word count is between 800-1000 words. Do not go over or under this word count range. Question 1 – Define the element of craft you’ve chosen (approx. 50 words) Question 2 – Analyse how the chosen craft element functions in the excerpt. What effect did the author achieve, and why did it work? (Approx. 500 words) Question 3 – Explain how studying this craft example can inform your own creative practice. (Approx. 100 words) Question 4 –Write an original paragraph to demonstrate how you have learned to use this craft element in your own writing. (Approx. 250 words) Question 5 – Write a short original writing exercise which teaches someone how to learn this craft element. (Approx. 100 words) This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
TurnItIn Receipts:
Assignments for this course will be submitted electronically via Blackboard and using TurnItIn . Before submitting any assignments for this course you must ensure you have completed UQ's compulsory online Academic Integrity Tutorial.
When you successfully submit your assessment to TurnItIn you will see text confirming your submission is complete, before being redirected to your Assignment inbox. On this page you can:
View the name of the submitted file View date and time of the upload Resubmit your paper (if necessary) Download your submitted paper Download digital receipt.
If you cannot see your submission in your Assignment inbox you should regard your submission as unsuccessful. Students are responsible for retaining evidence of submission by the due date for all assessment items, in the required form (e.g. screenshot, email, photo, and an unaltered copy of submitted work).
If the submission was not successful:
Note the error message (preferably take a screenshot). Go to your assignment page and see if it is possible to submit again. If you cannot submit again email your course coordinator immediately with the assignment attached.
Please visit this webpage for further advice on how to submit your TurnItIn assignment .
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
10% penalty is added every 24 hours that a submission is late

assessment_details.title: Genre Fiction Manuscript
assessment_details.due_date: 28/05/2026 4:00 pm Submit via TurnItIn through Blackboard
assessment_details.other_conditions: Student specific. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: AI-use must be acknowledged and referenced. Please add your AI statement to the cover sheet as per previous assessments. This should include the AI chart and your statement (and references if relevant). Task description Write an opening of a novel, in the genre of the student's choice, 1800-2000 words. Within this wordcount you must: * Define the genre conventions in a separate introductory paragraph (keep it brief/bullet points are fine) * finish the scene * include an outline of the remaining scenes in the chapter (1-2 lines per scene) * remain within the allowable word count range (this may require you to tighten what you have written) * this must be the final draft of the work started in Week 1 (you cannot submit a different story with different characters/plot) Your opening scenes should do the following: Conflict o It must have some foreshadowing or link to the broader plot. o It must have a ‘hook’ that leads into the next chapter/scene. Character o It must establish the character’s motivation. o It must involve dialogue between at least two ‘in-scene’ characters at some point Context/Setting o It must have a concrete setting that is introduced and explored in some way. o The setting must in some way be researched or designed. Structure o It must have at least two scenes. o The ending of the chapter should coincide with the ending of submitted sample. Format: * Double spaced * In a 12 point font (preferably a serif font e.g. Times) * Have margins of at least 2.5cm all around * Indent the first line of every paragraph * Use only one hard return (enter key) between paragraphs * If you are in doubt, look at a published book and lay out just like that, but double spaced * Incorrect layout will lose you marks on presentation *Going over or under the word count range will gain a penalty from the rubric for not following guidelines This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_resources.library_resources: Find the required and recommended resources for this course on the UQ Library website.

learning_activities.topic: Week 1: Introduction - Getting into the Genre Groove Why is Revision the core focus in this course? Overview of course, genre fiction, assessment and more
Week 1 Tutorial How to workshop. How to use PeerMark - a practical demonstration of this required technology; What is a novel and how is a novel structured? Learning outcomes: L01, L02
Week 2: The Writer as Scholar - Using Research to inform Genre Fiction Researching content and form. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04
Week 2 Tutorial IMPORTANT: Craft analysis preparation & Homework In tutorial assessment: Logbook Prompt 1. Learning outcomes: L02, L03
Week 3: Who's Talking Anyway? Point of View and Voice Character, Voice, Point of View, Narrators Learning outcomes: L01, L03
Week 3 Tutorial In tutorial assessment with Logbook Prompt 2; Working on your drafts; writing exercises Learning outcomes: L03, L04

Week 4 Guest Lecture: The Friction Behind the Fiction - Using Conflict to Power Narratives Guest Lecturer, Martine Kropowski, author of Left Behind and Everywhere We Look. We'll be focussing on plotting & conflict. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
Week 4 Tutorial In tutorial assessment with Logbook Prompt 3; Logbook feedback session; Peer Workshopping; Writing exercises Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04
Week 5: Imagined Landscapes, Real Impact - Setting as a Narrative Engine Worldbuilding, setting & place; Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
Week 5 Tutorial In tutorial assessment with Logbook prompt 4; peer workshopping; writing exercises Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04
Week 6: Independent Writing Week Time to do some major revision - and perhaps finish the course reading list. No lecture or tutorials Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04
Week 6 Independent Writing Week Use this time to revise your drafts with lessons learned to date. No tutorial. Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04

Week 7: Where the Walls Breathe - Making Tension come Alive Guest speaker is Gillian Hagenus, 2026 winner of the Glendower Award (Queensland Literary Awards) for an emerging writer. Tension-building techniques; how to revise them Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
Week 7 Tutorial Assessment: Logbook Prompt 5; Workshopping; writing exercises Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04
Week 8: Sensory Frontiers - Crafting Meaning with Themes, Symbolism and Imagery Imagery and similar craft techniques Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
Week 8: Tutorial Writing exercises; Logbook prompt 6; workshopping Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04

Week 9: The Long Game - How Writing Fuels both Story and Career Success How to sustain your writing over the long term; identifying how your writing and this course translates to the real world; employability skills Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
Week 9: Tutorial Logbook Prompt 7; Workshopping; Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04
Week 10: Independent Writing Week No tutorials or lecture. Draft revision intensive! Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04
Week 10: Independent Writing Week Redraft your final assessment Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04
Week 11: Draft 2.0 - Level Up Your Writing Through Copyediting Editing your draft; Final assessment discussion Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
Week 11: Tutorial Structure check; Workshopping; Logbook Prompt 8 Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04
Week 12: Publisher Guest Lecturer Our guest lecturer is the Publisher of Riveted Press, Rowena Beresford. Learning outcomes: L01
Week 12: Tutorial Final workshopping; tutor one-on-one, editing swap, Survey Party! Q&A. Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04
Week 13: Independent Writing Week There are no lectures or tutorials in week 13. Use this week to focus on your writing and submit by the deadline Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure AI for Assessment Guide Recording of Teaching Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library . Course guidelines Communication Expectations While you are a student at UQ, all communication must be conducted according to the UQ Student Code of Conduct . The UQ Library has a helpful Communicate and collaborate online module . Email is the primary way for you to send messages to, and receive information from, the School and our staff. You must use your UQ email address (not a private address) to communicate with staff. You should add a clear subject line, including course code, and a 2-3 word statement. You can send email at any time, however please do not expect responses outside normal working hours (Monday to Friday from ~8am to ~5pm). Emails that constitute bullying, harassment or discrimination against staff contravene the Student Code of Conduct. Emails like this will be reported to the University, and the matter will be pursued as misconduct. Learning activities
